---
title: "ENVS 193DS Homework 3"
date: "May 31, 2026" 
author: "Danielle Sur" 
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
---

# Set up

```{r}
#| message: false

# loaded in packages
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(here)
library(readxl)

# read in data and saved as object called `kelp`
kelp   <- readr::read_csv(here::here("data","temp-kelp.csv"))
# read in my data and saved as object called 'rxntime'
rxntime <- readr::read_csv(here::here("data","rxn-time.csv"))
```

# Problem 1. Giant kelp fronds

## a. An appropriate test

Pearson's and Spearman rank correlation can be used to test the strength of the relationship between temperature and giant kelp frond elongation rate. Whereas Pearson's r would indicate the strength and direction of linear association between the two variables (temperature and kelp elongation rate), Spearman's rho indicates the monotonic relationship between variables.

## b. Create a visualization

```{r}
# base layer: ggplot
ggplot(kelp,
       # x-axis: temperature
       # y-axis: kelp elongation
       aes(x = temp_c,
           y = kelp_elong)) +
  # added raw data points with custom color
  geom_point(color = "dodgerblue") +
  # relabeled x and y axes
  labs(x = "Temperature (°C)",
       y = "Kelp Elongation (cm/day)") +
  # changed default theme
  theme_minimal()
```

## c. Check your assumptions and run your test

### Check your assumptions

#### Scatter plot

```{r}
# base layer: ggplot
ggplot(kelp,
       # x-axis: temperature
       # y-axis: kelp elongation
       aes(x = temp_c,
           y = kelp_elong)) +
  # added raw data points with custom color
  geom_point(color = "red4") +
  # relabeled x and y axes
  labs(x = "Temperature (°C)",
       y = "Kelp Elongation (cm/day)") +
  # changed default theme
  theme_minimal()
```

#### QQ plot for temperature

```{r}
# base layer: ggplot
ggplot(data = kelp,
       # y-axis: temperature
       mapping = aes(sample = temp_c)) +
  # first later: reference line
  geom_qq_line(color = "hotpink") +
  # second layer: QQ points
  geom_qq()
```

#### QQ plot for kelp elongation rate

```{r}
# base layer: ggplot
ggplot(data = kelp,
       # y-axis: kelp elongation
       mapping = aes(sample = kelp_elong)) +
  # first later: reference line
  geom_qq_line(color = "yellow4") +
  # second layer: QQ points
  geom_qq()
```

A visual check of the kelp data on a scatter plot verified that there was a linear relationship between variables (plotted data appears to follow a linear trend). Normality of variables (temperature and kelp elongation rate) were checked with two separate QQ plots, respectively. Both display data points that mostly adhere to the line, so normality can be assumed for the data of both variables. Independent observations and continuous variables are also assumed.

### Run test

```{r}
# Pearson's correlation test
cor.test(kelp$temp_c, kelp$kelp_elong,
         method = "pearson")
```

## d. Results communication

I used a Pearson's correlation test to evaluate the strength of the relationship between temperature and giant kelp frond elongation rate because it tests the strength and direction of the relationship between two variables (in this case, temperature and kelp frond elongation rate). We found a moderate negative relationship between temperature and kelp frond elongation rate (Pearson's r = -0.69, t(30) = -5.2, p = 0.00001, $\alpha$ = 0.05).

## e. Test implications

The results of this test imply that increasing ocean temperatures are moderately associated with decreasing giant kelp frond elongation rates. Climate change has been altering the physical and chemical properties of oceans globally; since kelp forests provide various ecosystem services to humans and the world at large, it may be worthwhile to further investigate the impacts of rising temperatures on their growth.

## f. Double check your own work

```{r}
# Spearman's correlation test
cor.test(kelp$temp_c, kelp$kelp_elong,
         method = "spearman",
         exact = FALSE) # approx p-value for moderate sample size
```

With Pearson's correlation test, we found a strong negative relationship between temperature and kelp elongation rate (Pearson's r = -0.69, t(30) = -5.2, p = 0.00001, $\alpha$ = 0.05).

Spearman's correlation test leads to the same conclusion; we found a strong negative relationship between temperature and kelp elongation rate (Spearman $\rho$ = -0.69, S = 9216.1, p = 0.00001, $\alpha$ = 0.05).

Spearman's correlation tests the monotonic relationship between kelp elongation and temperature. The resulting correlation coefficient is negative and significant, meaning that with either test, we find that there is a significant, negative relationship between temperature and kelp frond elongation rates.

# Problem 2. Personal data 

## a. Updating your visualizations

### Cleaning data

```{r}
# created a new object from rxntime
rxntime_type <- rxntime |> 
  # cleaned column names
 clean_names() |> 
  # selected columns of interest
  select(exercise_type, reaction_time_avg_ms) |> 
  # removed missing values
  drop_na(exercise_type, reaction_time_avg_ms) |> 
  # capitalized existing exercise type categories
  mutate(exercise_type = case_when(
    exercise_type == "biking" ~ "Biking",
    exercise_type == "volleyball" ~ "Volleyball",
    exercise_type == "walking" ~ "Walking"
  ))
```

```{r}
# created a new object from rxntime
rxntime_clean <- rxntime |> 
  # cleaned column names
 clean_names() |> 
  # selected columns of interest
  select(exercise_duration_min, reaction_time_avg_ms) |>
  # removed missing values
  drop_na(exercise_duration_min, reaction_time_avg_ms)
```

### Figure 1. Exercise type and reaction time

```{r}
# base layer: ggplot
ggplot(data = rxntime_type,
       # x-axis: exercise type
       # y-axis: average reaction time in milliseconds
       # color by exercise type
       mapping = aes(x = "Exercise Type",
                     y = "Average Reaction Time (ms)",
                     color = exercise_type)) +
  # first layer: data points
  geom_point(data = rxntime_type,
             aes(x = exercise_type,
                 y = reaction_time_avg_ms,
                 size = 3)) +
  # changed theme
  theme_minimal() +
  # removed legend
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.background = element_blank(), # removing panel background
        panel.grid = element_blank(), # removing panel grid
        axis.ticks = element_blank() # removing axis ticks)
  ) +
  # relabeled x- and y- axes, adding title
  labs(title = "Exercise Type and Reaction Time",
       subtitle = "May 29, 2026",
       x = "Exercise Type",
       y = "Average Reaction Time (ms)") +
  # custom colors
  scale_color_manual(values =
                       c("Biking" = "magenta",
                         "Volleyball" = "purple3",
                         "Walking" = "blue"))
```

### Figure 2. Exercise duration and reaction time

```{r}
# base layer: ggplot
ggplot(data = rxntime_clean,
       # x-axis: exercise duration in minutes
       # y-axis: average reaction time in milliseconds
       # color
       mapping = aes(x = "Exercise Type",
                     y = "Average Reaction Time (ms)")) +
  # first layer: data points
  geom_point(data = rxntime_clean,
             aes(x = exercise_duration_min,
                 y = reaction_time_avg_ms,
                 size = 3,
                 color = "hotpink")) +
  # changed theme
  theme_minimal() +
  # removed legend
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.background = element_blank(), # removing panel background
        panel.grid = element_blank(), # removing panel grid
        axis.ticks = element_blank(), # removing axis ticks
    ) +
  # relabeled x- and y- axes, adding title
  labs(title = "Exercise Duration and Reaction Time",
       subtitle = "May 29, 2026",
       x = "Exercise Duration (min)",
       y = "Average Reaction Time (ms)") +
  # custom colors
  scale_color_manual(values = "hotpink")
```

## b. Captions

**Figure 1. Mean reaction times across biking, volleyball, and walking exercises.**
Points represent observations of mean reaction time (ms) after different kinds of exercise (pink: Biking, purple: volleyball, blue: walking). Data from my personal observations. Data accessed 2026-05-29. 

**Figure 2. Mean reaction times and exercise duration.**
Points represent observations of mean reaction time (ms) in relation to exercise duration (min). Data from my personal observations. Data accessed 2026-05-29.

# Problem 3. Affective visualization

## a. Describe in words what an affective visualization could look like for your personal data.

Taking inspiration from the Dear Data project, I could make a visual where each observation is represented by an icon (a stick figure for walking, a volleyball, and a bike tire for biking). Additional doodles such as a water droplet or a star can indicate whether water was consumed throughout exercise, or if an exercise exceeded a certain amount of time. Colors can be used to indicate levels of social interaction.

## b. Create a sketch (on paper) of your idea.

```{r fig-screenshot-1, echo=FALSE, out.width='400px'}
knitr::include_graphics("C:/github/ENVS-193DS_homework-03/images/sketch.png")
```

## c. Make a draft of your visualization.

```{r fig-screenshot-2, echo=FALSE, out.width='400px'}
knitr::include_graphics("C:/github/ENVS-193DS_homework-03/images/draft.png")
```

## d. Write an artist statement.

This is a visualization of the exercises I have done throughout the spring quarter. The icons arranged in an array of thirty observations each represent a type of exercise (walking, biking, or volleyball) while indicating factors such as socialization with other people and hydration. I was inspired most by the Dear Data project; especially by how colors and shapes were used not only to embellish the visualization but to tell a story. I created this draft with a sheet of A4 paper, a pen, and a couple highlighters. It is hand-drawn.

## e. Prep your materials to share in class.

View presentation slides [here](https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1JCIGFIhqZ6NhE6zjIz3g13vsWye4e50MgZmk1uO-mqw/edit?usp=sharing).

# Problem 4. Statistical critique

## a. Revisit and summarize

Pearson's R tests were used in this experiment to determine whether the relationship between coral cover and sea urchin density across all six studied reefs (Malindi, Watamu, Vipingo, Kanamai, Bamburi, and Diani) was significant.

```{r fig-screenshot-3, echo=FALSE, out.width='400px'}
knitr::include_graphics("C:/github/ENVS-193DS_homework-03/images/cc_sud.png")
```

## b. Visual clarity

The statistics are clearly represented in this linear model; the x- and y-axes are in logical positions while the line of best fit, the r-value, p-value, model predictions (via the line of best fit), and underlying data points are present.

## c. Aesthetic clarity

The data:ink ratio appears to be low. Visual clutter of the data is kept to a minimum by only including values that are necessary to understand it. This linear regression model is, structurally, quite neat as it is used to display the relationship between two variables. There is a slight sense of compaction, but that can be attributed to the densely-packed nature of the paper itself.

## d. Recommendations

I would try to further visually separate the variables (coral cover and sea urchin density) from their units. Currently, they are separated by a comma, which may be easily overlooked by quick eyes and potentially confuse the reader. If this paper were to be made available not just in black print, I think that the raw data and trend line would look aesthetically pleasing in different colors (and allow them to stand out visually from the rest of the figure).
